Concepts/Broad/Base.title: Base
Concepts/Broad/Base.content:
General
Imagine that you have a direct line outward from the center of your torso. That is zone where you can influence your opponent the most. Typically it is seen as advantageous if your center line is towards opponent, but theirs is pointing elsewhere.
Notable exception is Never flat on back -rule
Background
Previously this was called centerline, but the Biernacki version of is a neat way of conceptualizing it.
Typically base is referring to a place on the mat where you have a limb that prevents you from tipping over, but I don’t think that this necessarily as important as a concept as the focus on direction where you can apply force.

Concepts/Broad/Flat-on-back.title: Flat on back
Concepts/Broad/Flat-on-back.content:
If opponent is trying to lift you from your back a general defence to make yourself heavier is the straighten the back against the floor.
Not sure how it functions, but I imagine lifting a rope is easier at the start as the weight being lifted is lighter, opposed to plank weighting the same since you need to lift the entire amount at once. In BJJ this matters as if the opponent has easy time at the start they will already have the positional advantage. As such it makes it a lot easier to make it more difficult for them from the get go.

Concepts/Broad/Positions-of-strength.title: Positions of strength
Concepts/Broad/Positions-of-strength.content:
Inner control
In many positions like Standing and Closed guard you can have a lot superior control over the opponent when you hold the grips that are on the inner side. This can be even applied to Open guard, and is particularly visible in the Butterfly guard, where you have 2 inner hooks to control opponent and prevent passing.
Upper control
Typically in Standing if your grips are above the grips of the opponent you have slightly more advantageous position. This conceptually also applies to Dogfight scenarios where the higher your hips are the more advantage you have.
Turns out gravity is no insignificant part of the game.

Concepts/Broad/Posture.title: Posture
Concepts/Broad/Posture.content:
General
You can severely limit your opponents effectiveness by turning their head in other direction. Most common case is the Side control where you give them Crossface in order to force their head in different direction than rest of their body.
Human body is apparently at it’s strongest when your spine is aligned in a form that is not twisted. Note that this does not seem to be impacted by leaning forward as much as that is still in line with the
This is a concept and naming obtained from Biernacki and he is the one calling it primarily posture. Typically posture is used to refer to just having your back straight, but it seems to make a lot more sense when it is applied to how twisted the spine is.

Concepts/Broad/Pressure.title: Pressure
Concepts/Broad/Pressure.content:
When in Knee on belly, Turtle, Mount your need to remember to apply constant pressure to prevent opponent from moving and giving them ground for free.
This can be hard to achieve as there is lot to do while in these positions, but this should be a constant reminder to keep the pressure on constantly.
Also has mental aspect as it doesn’t give opponent the breathing room and if they are made uncomfortable they might be getting exhausted quicker

Concepts/Elbow-block.title: Elbow block
Concepts/Elbow-block.content: When being in a situation where your guard is being passed via a sudden sideways movement you should automatically put your elbow under the knee that is about to hit the floor. This reaction makes all afterward moves a lot easier to pull off

Concepts/Knee-Shield.title: Knee Shield
Concepts/Knee-Shield.content:
General defensive tool that means any situation where you have your elbow glued to the same sides knee. This forms a strong blocking posture on that side.
Problem is that you are essentially using two limbs in purely defensive mode, which you need to move away from in order to attack

Concepts/Specific/Back-escape-direction.title: Back escape direction
Concepts/Specific/Back-escape-direction.content: Always escape away from the choking hand.

Concepts/Specific/Never-flat-on-back.title: Never flat on back
Concepts/Specific/Never-flat-on-back.content:
Ideally when on the bottom on your back every movement should start with movement to a slight side tilt. This can even apply to a Closed guard as it makes the Triangle bit easier
The value of this concept comes from the wide usage of it

Defence/Back-defence.title: Back defence
Defence/Back-defence.content:
Back defense is fun due to the simplicity of it:
Handfighting
Prevent opponent from establishing strong grips and more importantly prevent them from isolating an arm.
Pressure
Alleviate pressure by pushing backwards so the opponent is no longer sitting and instead with their back on the ground
Head placement
Attempt to place opponents head between yours  and the choking arm
Escape direction
Escape direction is away from the choking arm

Defence/Granby-roll.title: Granby roll
Defence/Granby-roll.content: When in Turtle and opponent is on side and not applying constant pressure you can roll over shoulder that is on the opponents side and regain Open guard.

Defence/Knee-on-belly-defence.title: Knee on belly defence
Defence/Knee-on-belly-defence.content: Try Never flat on back -idea to see if the knee can be loosened

Defence/Lapel-choke-defence.title: Lapel choke defence
Defence/Lapel-choke-defence.content: When opponent has from behind your lapel gripped they are always trying to move their elbow behind your back and you should aim to eliminate that movement, either by handfighting or going to a side.

Defence/Lasso-defence.title: Lasso defence
Defence/Lasso-defence.content:
When opponent has you on Lasso guard the options are either to move backward, which allows you to hopefully get the shin out of your arm pit. If this happens you can either
Shoulder roll
Rotate your shoulder to put the opponents shin on “outside”.
Stacking
On the side where the lasso is rotate your torso towards opponent. This doesn’t allow opponent to control distance and the lasso loses a lot of it’s power. Usually leading to opponent changing grips

Defence/Lockdown-defence.title: Lockdown defence
Defence/Lockdown-defence.content: Based on using your free leg to push down the opponents locking legs. Not particularly flashy or special

Defence/Mount-Defence.title: Mount Defence
Defence/Mount-Defence.content:
Always start with getting on side. See: Never flat on back
Elbow on opponents thigh, with hand crossed and cupped over other thigh
Try to use both legs to control and limit opponents leg movement on the cupping hand’s side. (Also the side where you are leaning to)

Goal is to move to a Half guard

Defence/Side-control-defence.title: Side control defence
Defence/Side-control-defence.content:
As the side control is a position there is little use in defending it, as you should aim to perform a sweep or escape.
Both sweep options start with obtaining the underhook on your lower arm on the opponents armpit near their waist.
Towards the opponent
This way is when you use the sweep to push the opponent enough to gain hold of their leg from a roughly behind position. This can be used as a part of sweep by blocking their closer leg off with your knee and their far leg with a grip on their pants. After you have their legs entangled, you can simply push them on their side and hopefully obtain a sidecontrol.
Slither away
Same underhook start. This time use legs in a pendulum fashion to gain momentum, first towards the opponents waist quickly swithing the momentum towards their shoulders and using the momentum to “slither” backwards. You should be in a “face off”ish situation, where you have a control of their arm.
This can lead to a submission where your:

Keep the opponents arm entangled
Obtain a lapel grip on your free arm
Move head to the opponents torsos side
Roll over
Lapel grip applies the pressure, while the other arm should be extended by placing your arm as a lock which is kept in place by your wrist under their neck. See Side control & Drills

Defence/Turtle-defence.title: Turtle defence
Defence/Turtle-defence.content:
Granby roll towards the opponent or use unnamed move where you:

Take outer leg and raise posture
Push your leg through under yourself and opponent to gain Open guard

Out of the two Granby is flashier and more reliable
When opponent is on your front arc you can also try to:

Push upwards with both hands
Raise one leg from kneeling
Remaining leg is stepped under out under your opponent towards the opening created by raised leg
Movement reversed to quickly take Back

Drills--and--Practises/Guard-retention-d.title: Guard retention drills
Drills--and--Practises/Guard-retention-d.content:
Rotating with opponent
On back with legs on defensive posture. Rotate with elbows. This is particularly easy to practice at home as well.
TIP: Keep your knees as close to your chest as possible, so waist is elevated making the rotating easier.
Guard retention with limitations
Only arms
Only legs

Drills--and--Practises/Neck-related.title: Neck related
Drills--and--Practises/Neck-related.content:
General
These are warm ups that aim to strengthen the neck and support
Moves
Handstand
Start of by posting head and hands on mat. Arms in 90 degree angle to support your knees, which you’ll be placing there initially. Then afterwards you can improve by lifting the legs slowly and extend them
Back and forth
Goal is to be able to move between bridge and all fours by swinging the legs over you.
Start in all fours so that only arms and legs are touching the mat. Try to move so you can jump the legs over and land in a bridge. Similarly you need to revert this and repeat as possible.
Clock rotation
Plant hands and head similarly to in handstand. Then start moving in clockwise or counter clockwise motion with your legs. Eventually you will arrive in a state where you cannot move further without moving your arms and head, but instead step your leg over and continue the motion in a Bridge -like form. Similarly you should continue and step again to get back to starting position

Drills--and--Practises/Side-shimmy-pract.title: Side shimmy practice
Drills--and--Practises/Side-shimmy-pract.content: The usual sunday thing. Find a proper name and explanation for this

Empty_Pages.title: Empty_Pages
Empty_Pages.content:
Empty Notes

Figure-four grip
Gogoplata
Kimura
Canto choke
North south
Rubber guard
Double leg

Grip-breaks/Collar-grip-break-downward.title: Collar grip break downward
Grip-breaks/Collar-grip-break-downward.content:
Collar Grip breaks downwards

Two hand on the top pulling downward
Raise posture of increase effectiveness

Grip-breaks/Cross-collar-grip-break-to-t.filePath: Grip breaks/Cross collar grip break to takedown.md
Grip-breaks/Cross-collar-grip-break-to-t.title: Cross collar grip break to takedown
Grip-breaks/Cross-collar-grip-break-to-t.content:
Cross collar grip “past” shoulder

Cross collar grip and other hand grabs the elbow
Goal is to pull the gripping arm over the shoulder, removing grip and creating forward momentum “past” you

You can retain the grip on the


“Kick” with leg to control distance and transition to a general Takedown

Grip-breaks/Grip-break-on-upper-back.filePath: Grip breaks/Grip break on upper back.md
Grip-breaks/Grip-break-on-upper-back.title: Grip break on upper back
Grip-breaks/Grip-break-on-upper-back.content:
Opponents grip on upper back

Use two hands to move arm over your head
Afterwards the grip is easy to remove by a quick snap

Grip-breaks/Grip-breaks.title: Grip breaks
Grip-breaks/Grip-breaks.content: Generally grip breaks are not something to categorize separately, but there are situations where breaking a grip can be easily transitioned to something else.

Grips/Gable-grip.title: Gable grip
Grips/Gable-grip.content: Also known as palm to palm. Particularly good for defence against Armbar

Grips/Gift-wrap.title: Gift wrap
Grips/Gift-wrap.content:
Gift wrap is the act of getting the opponents arm around their neck and locking it there. This can typically be employed from Side control or Mount
Opponent can defend the gift wrap by having hand close to neck, which leaves them vulnerable to Americana. This can be done by choosing available arm, pushing it to the ground and then locking the Figure-four grip. Possible variation is that opponent defends with their free arm, leaving them open for gift wrap again.
Gift wrap is typically done on the far side of the opponent, so you can use your neck in the control, while keeping pressure on their torso.
If done while opponent is on their back can lead to S Mount

Grips/Lego-grip,-C-cup-grip.title: Lego grip, C-cup grip
Grips/Lego-grip,-C-cup-grip.content: Named due to similarity in how the grip is held. Not really a grip as such, but rather a way to target opponent limb and keep at distance with a Stiff arm.

Grips/Overhook.title: Overhook
Grips/Overhook.content:
Naming
Consider your hooking arm from your point of view. If the arm is angling downward then the the move in question is overhook. A hook over the limb.
Not to be confused with Underhook

Grips/S-grip.title: S grip
Grips/S-grip.content: Interlock the fingers in S -form. Easily broken and not particularly good

Grips/Seatbelt-grip.title: Seatbelt grip
Grips/Seatbelt-grip.content: One hand in S grip -like form over the choking hand under additional protection.

Grips/Sleeve-and-collar-grip.title: Sleeve and collar grip
Grips/Sleeve-and-collar-grip.content:
Grabbing the sleeve and collar of the opponent. This offers great options as means of control and usually needs to be quickly broken by the opponent
Value is in the ability to control opponent on different “levels” as opposed to Spider guard 1, allowing no single trivializing solution. Additionally you can increase control by Posting legs on opponents waist.

Grips/Thumb-and-cuff-grip.title: Thumb and cuff grip
Grips/Thumb-and-cuff-grip.content:
Description
Grip where you have only the thumb inside the lapel or similar clothing.


                  
                  Legality 
                  
                


It is prohibited to have four fingers inside opponents clothing



Variations
Thumb in
Thumb in, four fingers out
Cuff grip
Essentially the four finger grip, but in order to remain legal the clothing needs to be folded as the fold itself can be grabbed with four fingers.

Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Frame.title: Frame
Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Frame.content:
Frame is any limb preventing opponent from closing the distance and can often be used to push to gain further distance between you.
Can be dine with Stiff arm, but is not a reguirement

Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Instep.title: Instep
Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Instep.content: The instep is the arched part of the top of the foot between the toes and the ankle.

Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Posting.title: Posting
Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Posting.content: Posting is the act of placing a limb on the ground to stabilize your Posture. This is often used in move descriptions to identify which limbs are most vulnerable for attacks to obtain sweeps

Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Stiff-arm.title: Stiff arm
Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Stiff-arm.content: Arm that is fully extended. Valuable for framing against opponents body pressure, but also vulnerable to locks and arm extension should be used only when needed

Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Ulna.title: Ulna
Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Ulna.content: Long bone in the forearm stretching from the elbow to the wrist. It is on the same side of the forearm as the little finger.Guard retention drills

Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Underhook.title: Underhook
Miscellaneous/Anatomy/Underhook.content:
Naming
Consider your hooking arm from your point of view. If the arm is angling upward then the the move in question is underhook. A hook under the limb.
Not to be confused with Overhook
General term for hooking the arm under the opponents armpit. This is a very strong general purpose term and is applied in plenty of moves and positions

Miscellaneous/GI/Gi.title: Gi
Miscellaneous/GI/Gi.content:
General
Jujutsu’s garment that is used unless participating in no-gi or “lukkopaini”.
Parts
Important parts to note for grips are Sleeve, Collar, Lapel and Belt
Ownership history
Cheapest Groundforce
Served well as my starting gi since starting 2023 until current date
Tatami Estilo Gold Label
As the first gi is still serving faithfully I decided to spend a bit more on the expensive Tatami one. It is supposedly heavier with the 550GSM, but feels pretty light to me.
It is also compliant with IBJJF 2024 rules regarding the gi’s.

Miscellaneous/GI/Lapel.title: Lapel
Miscellaneous/GI/Lapel.content:
General
Usually in clothing the lapel is the “chest” area, but in BJJ it is referring to the end of the clothing below the belt. As it is usually tucked under the belt this requires a lot of work to free and then to use.
Grips
Fist grip is probably the most solid one in most of the cases
Synergies

Miscellaneous/GI/Sleeve.title: Sleeve
Miscellaneous/GI/Sleeve.content:
General
Sleeves are essential part of many grips and holds and they give superior pulling capabilities. Many moves aim to trap the sleeve behind something in order to further cement the hold, as simple grips will eventually be broken
Grips
Very often held by Thumb and cuff grip and Fist grip
Synergies
Integral part of Spider guard and Lasso guard
Useful for Ezekiel

Passing/Closed-guard-figure-8.title: Closed guard figure 8
Passing/Closed-guard-figure-8.content:
Flow

Hands on opponents to prevent rising up
One knee on tailbone
Other knee on 8 o’clock
Raise posture and opponents grip should be eased by the leverage



                  
                  Critique 
                  
                

Issues arise that the side that does not have the posting 8 o’clock leg is vulnerable to a sweep.
Counter
Easy counter is to just elevate and move hip back to the centerline. If opponent is pushing heavily this can be attempted by rotating hips and “falling” from their leveraging knee.
Steps below are for the bottom player

Identify sweep prone side (4 o’clock side)
Isolate arm on that side
Raise and put hip on the opponents knees 4 o’clock side
Use hip and arm rotation to sweep opponent over

Passing/Closed-guard-standing-up.title: Closed guard standing up
Passing/Closed-guard-standing-up.content:
Three key steps are

Push opponent down on their waist to prevent taking grips
Keep posture up to prevent grips
Raise up to make holding Closed guard tiresome

When all three are accomplished you can start pushing open the legs to escape the guard
Be aware that the leg positioning can make you vulnerable to Lumberjack & Waiter

Passing/Double-ankle-grab-pass.title: Double ankle grab pass
Passing/Double-ankle-grab-pass.content:
Grip opponents knees and force them on a side to the floor
Defence for this is often a Lego grip, C-cup grip on the opponents forward arms wrist and then rotating backwards while maintaining Stiff arm. You can create distance and move to a better position.

Passing/Double-under.title: Double under
Passing/Double-under.content:
Process of the pass is to get two underhooks on the opponent, lock hands and put pressure. After this you can move towards a side and move legs with pressure on the opposite side, leading to a Side control
Can be defended against by Triangle, but with your own hand replacing the “choking” arm.

Put leg over opponents head
Grab under ankle with a Cup grip.

Keep arm in place as that is the choking element


Lock with second leg
Move to angle and squeeze

Alternative defense is grabbing opponents arms prior to him establishing the grip. Remember to Flat on back. Then you should be able to create enough space to fit legs in and Frame

Passing/Knee-slice.title: Knee slice
Passing/Knee-slice.content:
Hold opponents knee.
Move opponents leg to the floor and step over it with your own opposing knee.
If the opponent has their elbow under their leg, then abort and move backwards, as moving forward will just move you to a worse position “over” the mark

When being passed with a knee slice always or any guard passing that is using sudden movement to side consider Elbow block -idea

Passing/Leg-drag.title: Leg drag
Passing/Leg-drag.content:
Take hold of opponents leg with your inner hand on their ankle and outer on their knee. Then pull back, while moving forward to bypass the leg. Apply pressure to prevent putting the knee back in place.
Defense for leg drag is typically Stiff arm on opponents upper torso accompanied with your free hand blocking the momentum on the wrong direction. Think of it as applying your elbow to the mat quickly at and opposite angle to your opponent.
You should then move to side facing opponents and put your free leg in between to allow creating space and returning to guard.

Passing/Toreando.title: Toreando
Passing/Toreando.content:
Both hand grip on the opponents knees. Followed by a simultaneous movement to their side, while pushing both the knees to the ground
Defence for Toreando pass is putting your elbow under your lower knee, allowing you more mobility and then typically using your lower legs shin to move over the opponents mostly Stiff arm allowing you to position your leg on their bicep or check and push them back.
Note on the shin movement, that while it is possible to do it below the opponents arm they can block it with their elbow. Movement above is not easy to block, but can be tough depending on the opponents grip and arm length.

Positions/Back.title: Back
Positions/Back.content:
Top 3:
Rear naked choke
Armbar
Bow and arrow
Buying time
You can buy time by using Underhook arm to grab their armpit and then hold tightly. Just a temporary solution though
Moves
Straight jacket from back
Shirt variants
You can also use your shirt to alternative. Not a fan of these as they add a lot of complexity unnecessarily.
Tie around
Just tie the end of your shirt around their neck LMAO.
Baseball / Papercutterish
Feed your shirt end to your choking arm, fall to side of the choking arm and then finish?
See also Back defence

Positions/Closed-guard/Closed-guard.title: Closed guard
Positions/Closed-guard/Closed-guard.content:
Situation where you are on your back and opponent is deep between your legs. Despite the usual conventions of being on the back being bad, this is actually extremely good position and allows you to attack from guard, which is usually quite rate. Notable things to try out are:

Three sisters
Kimura
Gogoplata

Kimura

Open the guard
Rise up and cross arm behind opponents back

Right hand goes over opponents shoulder


Lift your back and get Posting on a stiff arm
Arm that is behind opponents back should grab the wrist in Figure-four grip

Closed guard Armbar

Have both opponents arms extended far towards your neck. You only need to control one, but the second cannot block your leg movement
Position legs on opponents waist to ease movement
The opposite leg to the arm your are attacking is put in the opponents armpit and crossed over their back

Pull opponent down


Get better angle so your leg, that is on opponents back is aligned with yourself
Put other leg on the side of their face

Close guard Triangle

Grab both wrists

One you pull towards how, this is the “choking arm”
Other you push towards opponents crotch


On the side of the choking arm put a leg on opponents waist and push against their shoulder to limit movement
On the other side move our leg over the opponents shoulder

This is only possible if the opponents hand is hidden and cannot block
Leg needs to be positioned so that the thigh is on opponents neck, and the shin on the back of their neck


Move first leg upward and “lock” the triangle in place

Closed guard Omoplata

Pick an arm and pull with opposite hand
Set free arm under opponents thigh to get 180 degree angle
Push legs straight forcing opponent to lower posture
Leg that crosses over their hand should be locked in place by the other leg

If opponent is standing
Lumberjack & Waiter
Unnamed variant

Move hip outside the opponents direct control
Grab the closer leg with Lego grip, C-cup grip
Crunch abs and opponent should fall

Closed guard passes

Positions/Closed-guard/High-guard.title: High guard
Positions/Closed-guard/High-guard.content:
Highguard is a close cousing of Closed guard with the exeption that it is used to isolate “out” a single shoulder
Typical way to get there is

Move one arm so the elbow is on opponents centerline
Same side leg on opponents waist
Push and angle to get other leg over their back
Move 1st leg between opponents shoulder and neck

After this legs can be crossed
Submissions after this are two variations of Armbar, Wristlock and some kind on pain submission which is based on the bone pushing on opponents arm in a downward motion. Not good for control so can be skipped.
If opponent pulls out an arm there is chance for Triangle

Positions/Combat-base.title: Combat base
Positions/Combat-base.content:
Combat base can be split into three distinct variations:
Combat base high
Sitting posture which can be used to maintain distance with your opponent when not wanting to fight Standing.
Usually one leg is on it’s side to provide stability and on the same side the arm is also Posting. On the other side your knee is elevated and your elbow is attached to create a shield.
Danger here is that if you Stiff arm the opponent they have possibility of flying Armbar
Combat base med
Fairly similar to regular standing combat base but the largest difference is the posting arm being on elbow instead of a straight arm
Adjacent or close to Half guard
Focus on these:

Keep opponent between your legs (see: Base)
Lower leg is a multi tool

Defensive for framing
Attacking the opponents leg
Mobility as it with your posting elbow are the two things moving you around
Additionally you can curl toes towards mat to “harden” your position to defend momentary attacks


Curled (defending) leg kept in the air with arm forming a knee shield

Heel close to ass
Instep curled upward


Arm on the knee shield is used to look for grips lapel or wrist/sleeve are excellent

Combat base low
Essentially a Open guard from your back. Only defensive options are available and you should start by limiting attach opportunities by moving to a side

Positions/Half-guard/Half-guard.title: Half guard
Positions/Half-guard/Half-guard.content:
Guard retention

Lock the leg
Extend body
Establish frame

Stiff arm if possible


Outer leg to return to better defensive posture

Sweeps
John Wayne sweep is also available and easy

Positions/Half-guard/Lockdown.title: Lockdown
Positions/Half-guard/Lockdown.content:
Quick way to think about is a triangle on a leg. Usable from Half guard. Can be used to stall, but is consided dishonorable. Should lead to a sweep by lifting opponents leg in a controlled manner.
Lockdown Sweep
Extending the leg after step 1. should extend the opponents leg and lift it off the mat
Lockdown defence

Positions/High-mount.title: High mount
Positions/High-mount.content: Advancement from regular mount where your knees are on opponents armpits. This should allow some finishes.

Positions/Knee-on-belly.title: Knee on belly
Positions/Knee-on-belly.content:
For defence see: Knee on belly defence
Grips on opponent lapel (upper torso) and waist (pants). One leg on solar plexus, ankle at opponents thigh. Other leg posted far to provide stability
If opponent pushes on upper torso with closer arm stretched :

Grab opponents closer wrist on sleeve and extend
Move leg from belly towards opponents armpit
Move posting leg over opponents head extended
Fall back and finalize Armbar

If opponent pushes on knee:

Use hand from waist and thread the needle created by opponents arm
Link arms and lift opponent to side
Move posted leg over opponents head and wedge on opponents back (prevents getting back on back)

You should be sitting on opponents head. Legs prevent the opponent from rotating to either Turtle or their back.
Can switch grip to complete Kimura, by setting up a Figure-four grip
If Kimura cannot be completed as the opponent has a grip on belt or similar

Lock the opponents arm in place by gripping their belt/pants/shirt
Use free arm to grab lapel (Under your own leg, in order to allow stepping over it)
Step back with the leg that is on the previously free arms side
Complete the Papercutter




Extend opponents arm
Fall back and finish the Armbar

Positions/Mount.title: Mount
Positions/Mount.content:
Highly advantageous position sitting on opponents waist. Typical next steps are advancing to a High mount, which is closer to the opponents armpits while allowing for some submissions?
Also see: Mount Defence

Positions/Open-guard/De-La-Riva-and-Reve.filePath: Positions/Open guard/De La Riva and Reverse De La Riva.md
Positions/Open-guard/De-La-Riva-and-Reve.title: De La Riva and Reverse De La Riva
Positions/Open-guard/De-La-Riva-and-Reve.content:
Description
Shorthand DLR and RDLR. Both are employed from back position where you entangle opponents leg from a side and circle it around their leg and rest the foot on their waist. The difference is largely that the normal variant has you circle the leg from outside and the reverse from inside.

Positions/Open-guard/K-guard.title: K guard
Positions/Open-guard/K-guard.content:
A guard where

One knee is crossed opponents chest
Other knee is on the mat and leg upward with ankle on opponents hip

Positions/Open-guard/Lasso-guard.title: Lasso guard
Positions/Open-guard/Lasso-guard.content:
Lasse is typically an extension to Sleeve and collar grip where you have opponents arm extended with a sleeve grip and then your leg intertwined over, under and in the armpit of the opponent. This is a very strong control and allows you to control opponents distance.
Lasso defence

Positions/Open-guard/Open-guard.title: Open guard
Positions/Open-guard/Open-guard.content:
General
Any general on the floor position where you have distance between you and your opponent should be improved immediately by either increasing the distance and getting to [Standing or establishing at minimum some sort of open guard.
Goal of the open guard is then to move to either Sweep or Closed guard

Positions/Open-guard/Spider-guard.title: Spider guard
Positions/Open-guard/Spider-guard.content:
Description
Guard based on grip on Sleeves and legs placed on opponents elbow creases. This allows you to push the opponent in directions quite freely, but the move is not easy to translate into next thing.
Grip details
Try to use Thumb and cuff grip, as that seems particularly good
Defence
Spider guard is easy to defend against by raising up and extending the arms, essentially making the legs not have a place to leverage for the push.

Positions/Open-guard/X-guards.title: X guards
Positions/Open-guard/X-guards.content:
Description
Quite vague concept having the legs form a “X” shape while controlling the opponents leg(s). Usually benefits from some control over the opponents ankle on at least one leg.
X guards are excellent grounds for many of the sweeps and really they should be in sweeps category just due to that.
Note that you need to be very close and almost already under the opponent to set the guard, so they are valuable reaction to opponent Passing your guard
Variants
Single X
Variant only focusing on one leg.

You have opponents right leg (on your left) in and Overhook on your armpit
Pendulum your left leg entirely behind opponents leg and back over so your shin is on their calf and your ankle on their waist
Right leg forms the “X” by simply pressing the shin to their calf

Sweep

Extend legs
Raise ass from ground
Opponents should lose balance and fall

X guard
Focusing on two legs simultaneously, although the name is quite misleading IMO. In this you need to have both your legs “inside”, making it a good transition from Butterfly guard.

Same grip on the opponents leg as in the single X
Left legs knee placed behind opponents left calf and the ankle on their waist on right side
Right legs shin is pressing against their right legs calf

Sweep

Extend legs
Raise ass from ground
Opponents should lose balance and fall

See also
Ankle-Waist-Shin for the lack of a better name is relatively close to X guards and can be considered when looking for alternatives

Positions/S-Mount.title: S Mount
Positions/S-Mount.content:
This mount gets it’s name from the position of the legs. Typically used from “side” position. Typical application is opposing leg in opponents armpit with the foot pointing towards opponents head, meanwhile the other leg’s knee is against opponents neck and foot pointing “downward”.
Opponent is heavily controlled allowing for Armbar, even possibly from elevated instead of the regular mat

Positions/Side-control.title: Side control
Positions/Side-control.content:
Can lead to Knee on belly, which has finishes
Can be progressed to Mount
You can also Gift wrap
Windshield wiper choke
Opponent on the receiving end is pushing toward with stiff arm. Lock the closer arm in place with a wrist locked behind their neck. This allows you to shimmy over to the opposite side with your toes.  Part of Drills
Can alternate sides by controlling the far arm and then using a Windshield wiper way to reposition. Particularly useful when opponent has managed to create some space and position on the side Never flat on back
Side control defence

Positions/Standing.title: Standing
Positions/Standing.content:
Match starting position. Leads to takedowns.
Focus on Underhook. Disregard grips on neck.
One practical way is to use grab both wrist and arm on the same side and drag downward in a quick motion. Alternatively can sidestep and reach for the Back
Fireman throw

Positions/Turtle/Flashy-Rolling-Backtake.title: Flashy Rolling Backtake
Positions/Turtle/Flashy-Rolling-Backtake.content:
Prerequisite: Opponent is in turtle and position you are on the rear

Grab Seatbelt grip
Plant your forehead on either 11 or 1 o’clock.

What should happen is that opponent should raise their hip and flip you off their back, but this time you are expecting this

Raise your legs over opponents
While flipping over tighten the seatbelt
Opponent should be flipped alongside you, allowing you to take Back

This movement is based on the conjunction of you Posting with your head, the momentum of the flip combined with the seatbelt tightened midway.
Uncreated pages
www.youtube.com/watch
Demonstrated variant would be done from side and taking the seatbelt from the opposite end. This would make it a lot more doable

Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-Crusifix-x-Pa.title: Turtle to Crusifix x Papercutter
Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-Crusifix-x-Pa.content:
Start is from Turtle “side”
Either bait the opponent to grab your inner leg by stepping near their shoulder or otherwise isolate it and lock with both legs
Grab lapel with outer hand
Inner hand grabs the wrist of far arm and moves it towards the opponents chest
Roll over opponent

You should have one of their arms locked in place by two legs, other hand is being controlled from wrist and collar arm should be able to apply Papercutter, like motion with wrist to finish choke.

Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-Crusifix.title: Turtle to Crusifix
Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-Crusifix.content: Force lower knee between knee shield. Reach over with lower arm and control far knee. Grab your closer knee and pull forward and isolate the arm. Armbar possible, but unreliable here. Alternatively roll over or pull back to get to crusifix. Theres a bunch on subs there

Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-Fatbike.title: Turtle to Fatbike
Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-Fatbike.content:
Requires one hook in place

Sink the hook deeper so the opponents thigh is controlled by your knee
Grab the far leg with both hands with Cup grip
Either roll forward or backward so you are under while controlling both legs

Backwards movement seemed more practical


Triangle/Lockdown first leg and retain control of the other with upper hand
Lower hand controls opponents neck
Move further up on their back
Sink the second hook in place

Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-Papercutter.title: Turtle to Papercutter
Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-Papercutter.content: Position at 2 o’clock and underhook on the same side to grab lapel. Other hand Thumb and cuff grips the neck. Jump to feet and raise opponent. Sweep opponent to their 4 o’clock (to their back) and rotate your lapel grip to finish Papercutter. Importance on flipping the opponent over to ease the movement for your lapel grip to transition to the finisher

Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-armbar.title: Turtle to armbar
Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-armbar.content:
Armbar

Pull opponent arm towards their back
Move outer leg over the opponents head to their armpit on opposite side

This can be retained as is. No need to extend later


Extend opponents arm
Fall backwards
Follow other leg to their neck

Leg over face


Finish Armbar

Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-sweep.title: Turtle to sweep
Positions/Turtle/Turtle-to-sweep.content:
Sweep
Particularly if at startup 4.1 you got a hook in you should apply this sweep.

Move opponents arm towards their chest
Roll in the direction of their exposed shoulder
Sink hooks in for Back control

Positions/Turtle/Turtle.title: Turtle
Positions/Turtle/Turtle.content:
Lessons learned from attacker side:
Pressure
Turtle defence
Variant of turtle is Panda
Always have the hook and rolling direction be opposite sides!
Starting positions

Rear
Side
Reverse (Head)

Attack targets

Isolate hand for a forward arc roll
Unbalance to gain a Hook

Attack patterns
Armbar / Sweep startup
Starting attacks:

Start from side of the turtling opponent
Grab opponent with two hands (Shoulder and waist for example)
Move towards either NW or NE depending on your side Base
Opponent posts hand

Posting hand can also create space for you to sink far left leg as a Hook to further secure the back (Not applicable if going for the Armbar)


Underhook and cup grip the wrist, which ideally is on the same side as you

After this you can opt for either

Turtle to armbar
Turtle to sweep

Turtle to Crusifix x Papercutter
Half Nelson > Roll > Backtake

Sink Underhook
Lock in place by Gable grip behind their neck
Roll towards opposite side to the underhook
Take Back

Turtle to Fatbike
Flashy Rolling Backtake

Recently_Updated.title: Recently_Updated
Recently_Updated.content:
Recently Updated Notes

index
Windshield wiper choke
Lockdown Sweep
Single leg
Ankle pick
Fireman throw
Arm drag
Double leg
Collar & Sleeve to takedown
John Wayne sweep

Submissions/Armbar.title: Armbar
Submissions/Armbar.content:
Generally useful and basic finisher which is based on extending the opponents are straight and pressuring the Elbow
Fast and quick rules:
Prevent rolling away, usually by having leg(s) over the opponents chest and face on different sides of the arm being locked in.
Have opponents thumb up, as if they can
Defence
Rotate arm to have the thumb pointing down
Roll to move to more advantageous position
Stack on the opponent.
Gripping the arm being attacked and pulling them together. This can be countered by spazzy movement as the grip cannot combat the opponents leg strenght.

Submissions/Baseball-choke.title: Baseball choke
Submissions/Baseball-choke.content:
Choke where you have a underhook on possibly turtling opponent and you decide to feed your shirt to your underhook. After the grip is established you grab the shirt from a higher point leading to a batting grip on your on shirt, which should be positioned tightly on opponents neck.
Legs are typically used in preventative fashion so opponent doesn’t just roll away.
Start at 2 o’clock

Feed shirt and grab with nearer underhook
Fall back a bit and use legs to prevent rolling and to apply pressure to the shirt pressing on neck

Baseball is essentially the same, but you grab the shirt tail with both hands to apply the pressure more deliberately

Submissions/Bow-and-arrow.title: Bow and arrow
Submissions/Bow-and-arrow.content:
Establish collar grip on opponents throat
Move to establish angle on the opposite side
Grab knee and put leg on waist to further the angle and finish the choke

This is a gi version and NoGi Bow and arrow works quite differently

Submissions/Buggy-choke.title: Buggy choke
Submissions/Buggy-choke.content:
From defending Side control

Push the arm near to opponents head on the neck side that is closer to you and raise it to the skies.
Then pendulum a leg and Lego grip, C-cup grip it from under and behind
Bring other arm there as well and change grip to Gable grip
Shoulderblades to the ground and crunch with abs

Submissions/Close-guard-overhook-stiff.title: Close guard overhook stiff
Submissions/Close-guard-overhook-stiff.content:
Description
The goal is to assume further control from Closed guard by obtaining a Overhook on opponents arm and straightening it. Grip the opponents closer Collar.


                  
                  Value 
                  
                


Value of this move comes from the flexibility where it allows you to control the distance extremely effectively by pushing and/or pulling



Moves
Simple submission
Push on shoulder and straighten the overhook
Omoplata
As you already have the arm controlled you can just put leg on opponents armpit and do an omoplata sweep
Basic sweep
Control on the arm blocks posting and gives easier time sweeping towards that direction

Obtain the overhook on left side

Angle torso to the right

Important to break opponents controlled arm from Posting


Left leg pushes opponent down by applying Kalapuikko
Other leg can be either pendulum’d to get momentum for a sweep

Alternatively loosen the overhook and simultaneously place leg over opponents head to finish Armbar

Submissions/Crusifix.title: Crusifix
Submissions/Crusifix.content:
Generally a move where you are on your back and the opponent is on his back above your. The name comes naturally from crusifixion as the opponents arms are tightly controller on the sides. Far extended one should be controller by two of your legs and the other with one of your arms. Helpless and unfortunate position to be in (for opponent).
One arm is sufficient to submit here in many ways
Attacking turtle to crusifix

Force lower knee between Knee Shield. Reach over with lower arm and control far knee. Grab your closer knee and pull forward and isolate the arm. Armbar possible, but unreliable here. Alternatively roll over or pull back to get to crusifix. Theres a bunch on subs there

Submissions/Kimura--and--Americana.title: Kimura & Americana
Submissions/Kimura--and--Americana.content:
Kimura and Americana are listed in a same post as the mechanics are essentially the same and the difference is opponent’s arms direction and possible applicable positions.
Difference
Americana has the opponent raising their hand towards their head, while Kimura is applicable when the opponents hand is towards their hip.
Mechanics
The submission targets the shoulder joint by isolating the opponent’s arm and applying rotational force.

Arm Isolation: The practitioner traps one of the opponent’s arms using a figure-four grip. This involves grabbing the opponent’s wrist with one hand and securing their arm by gripping your own wrist with the other hand.
Leveraging the Joint: With the figure-four grip in place, the practitioner rotates the opponent’s arm behind their back, creating torque on the shoulder joint. Properly secure the arm, so that the torque is created on shoulder and not all over the arm

Submissions/Papercutter.title: Papercutter
Submissions/Papercutter.content:
Generally speaking a choke applied from a side direction when opponent is on their back, being controlled from turning to their side.
Choke itself is applied by a grip on the lapel and a downward motion by the bone in the arm putting papercutter -like pressure on the neck

Submissions/Rear-naked-choke.title: Rear naked choke
Submissions/Rear-naked-choke.content:
RNC is initiated from a back position with a seat belt (the line should be from opponents waist to their opposing shoulder) with seatbelt grip.

Get hooks in: Goal is to solidify your back position using legs as hooks. NOTE: More control is gained by using the hooks as “high” as possible (nearer to opponents crotch)

If possible isolate opponents arm and trap it with your hook leg from the same side. The limit in defense is worth the trade you take in losing a hook.


Switch choking arm: Move choking arm to choking position where the bicep and forearm are positioned block carotid arteries. Therefore elbow should be roughly aligned with opponents center jaw
Secure the choking arm: Position choking arms blade in securing arms elbow pit. Close the lock by putting the securing arms hand on opposite shoulder.
Finish: Lean back, breath in and slowly tighten the hold

Submissions/Straight-ankle-lock.title: Straight ankle lock
Submissions/Straight-ankle-lock.content:
Description
Most commonplace leg attack that surprisingly is legal for all belts. Short and sweet version is that you need to have a opponents shin past your armpit, possibly in Shotgun grip. Both legs are posted on the opponent with the attacked leg being in between.
Escaping
These are just things to try out in order

Rise up and step on the leg. If it is being stood on the lock cannot work
Remove opponents legs, so they cannot push against your torso
Roll out, which can lead to a worsening of the situation

Submissions/Straight-jacket-from-back.title: Straight jacket from back
Submissions/Straight-jacket-from-back.content:
Straight jacket

Grab wrists of opponent
Fall on side
Push their top leg on top of the other
Grip the opposite arms (Pullable into a straight jacket)
Push the upper arm towards opponents chest from the wrist
Isolate the arm with your leg
Grab lapel and choke

Uncreated pages

Submissions/Three-sisters.title: Three sisters
Submissions/Three-sisters.content:
Three sisters are the:
Triangle
Omoplata
Armbar
As you can move between the three of them smoothly when performing them from the closed guard

Submissions/Triangle-Escape.title: Triangle Escape
Submissions/Triangle-Escape.content:
Post trapped arm straight to the ground on the opposite side of the opponents neck
Raise yourself with legs
Start rotating around the opponent to loosen the triangle

Submissions/Triangle.title: Triangle
Submissions/Triangle.content:
Triangle is one of the basic fundamental submissions and it is based on:

Isolate an opponents arm so it presses their neck
On the opposite side have your leg positioned on the neck as well and crossed over their back
Use your free leg to “lock” the triangle in place with knee pit on shin/ankle

Situations where you can perform triangle are various but a great place to start practicing is the Closed guard
Triangle Escape

Submissions/Two-grip-choke-on-turtle.title: Two grip choke on turtle
Submissions/Two-grip-choke-on-turtle.content:
Description
Basically you can feed the Lapel in a tricky way to force choke. This is reliant heavily on great control as otherwise the opponent will just roll away.
Steps

Start from opponents 2 o’clock. Hand closer to opponents waist takes Underhook and grabs Lapel
Other hand grips lapel at opponents necks at a medium distance to allow the pressure surface to be large enough for choke.
Underhook side’s leg is pushed against so the knee is raised against their hip. Prevents sideward rolling
Other leg is raised over their back to prevent rolling forward

Submissions/Windshield-wiper-choke.title: Windshield wiper choke
Submissions/Windshield-wiper-choke.content:
Naming 
                  
                


Windshield wiper is the leg movement to change position, and not the choke. Choke itself is more akin to Arm triangle



From side control the opponents far arm from bicep and get it slowly over their neck, fairly similary to Gift wrap (just without locking it)
Then proceed first to mount and move to the opposite side. Windshield refers to the movement of the legs in this step, as you should prevent opponent from controlling your legs.
When on the opposite side you should be able to create leverage on the opponents neck.
Uncreated pages

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/An.title: Ankle-Waist-Shin
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/An.content:
General
This is often named or described as “basic” sweep, and rather than have it be extremely well defined it is more related to the broad concept.

Left hand has grip on opponents ankle
Left leg is on their waist
Right leg is hooked to block shin to prevent backstepping
Extend the left leg and opponent will lose posture

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/De.filePath: Sweeps, throws & takedowns/Bottom/De La Riva sweep.md
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/De.title: De La Riva sweep
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/De.content:
General
See set up at De La Riva and Reverse De La Riva
There are two distinct DLR sweeps taught
Variations
DLR sweeps have multiple variations depending on which side you are moving the opponent to.
Forward
Start by having

DLR on left leg
Left hand on the same legs ankle
Right hand on the Collar on the left side
Right leg pushes opponents right knee right arm drags forward. Likely leads to opponent posting with both hands.

When they get back up you can move to the next backwards variant
Backward

Push DLR leg forward so it rests on the opponents far leg. This allows you to control both the legs to an extent
Left hand attempts to catch opponents far leg (the one not in DLR)

If opponent is too far you can lose the collar grip and instead grab Belt and pull so opponent needs to adjust their leg positioning


Once you have opponents far leg controlled from and their near leg between two of yours you can adjust the angle and move yourself to almost behind opponent.
Move your right leg as a “butterfly” hook on opponents thigh
Do the same for the other leg, leading to a “butterfly from behind”
Grab belt with both hands and lift opponent up leading you to take the Back

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/Jo.title: John Wayne sweep
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/Jo.content:
From Half guard

Notice the side on which you have the “outer” control
Block the opponents leg from posting on the “outer” side
Use hand to block opponent posting from outer side
Bridge directly up
Fall on the side

If opponent posts anyways you can “swim” under their armpit to reach for Back or force them to Turtle

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/La.title: Lasso sweep
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/La.content:
Establish the Spider guard 1 and loop leg around to get it in a lasso.
Use opposite sides arm to control opponents other hand and prevent Posting.
Use lasso sides leg to push against opponents hip
Use other leg to push other leg away, while pulling the arm gripped in step 2.

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/Lu.title: Lumberjack & Waiter
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/Lu.content:
Available when opponent is in your closed guard and has risen to standing. Lumberjack is based on getting one arm hooked behind opponents leg. This is the starting concept for the rest of the moves
Lumberjack
In a single move you should quickly move the arm in position and simultaneously use abs to force opponent downward. This could lead to a sweep. Easy to defend and unlikely to work unless opponent is surprised
Waiter
Variant where in addition to the leverage in lumberjack you use your other hand to raise yourself slightly from the mat as if carrying a tray. This gives a bit more leverage but feels weird initially.
Next up: Star sweep

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/Om.title: Omoplata
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/Om.content:
Omoplata is many things and most notably it is a part of Three sisters.
It can be primarily used as a sweep, but can be a submission, but the reliability isn’t all that high
At it’s core Omoplata is a situation where you have isolated your opponents arm and are lying on their side, while they are on all fours. This is often accompanied by your arm taking a grab of their thigh to gain better angle.
Sweep is generally performed by extending the locked legs > Forces opponent to lose posture and then falling on side dragging the opponent sideways over you.
From closed guard
There is also a variant where you can do omoplata sweep from closed guard.

Opponent is in high posture and you have an sleeve grip
Lumberjack & Waiter -like start and feed the sleeve to the lumberjack arm
Free hand grips lapel
Sweep the “opposite” leg in a broad arc over and lock with the other leg
Follow as with regular omoplata

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/St.title: Star sweep
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/St.content:
Same setup as in Lumberjack & Waiter. Prerequisite is also that opponent has lifted you so that shoulders aren’t on mat.
Star sweep is achieved by locking the arm in place and doing a wide motion with opposite side leg over the opponents head leading to a “roll” behind the opponent
NOTE: Head facing to the far side of the opponent so the roll doesn’t hurt your neck
After roll extend the sweeping leg and place it behind opponents angle and force leverage with your original arm grip (as in lumberjack and waiter) to force opponent backwards to the mat

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/Sw.title: Sweep
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Bottom/Sw.content:
General
Sweep is anything that sweeps the opponent your top position to a worse one. These are critical to know, because if you cannot sweep, then you are forced always to play defensive and can only employ submissions from Closed guard
Example

You are on bottom
Opponent is above, usually in a Closed guard or Open guard
One arm is prevented from posting
Raise your hip by Bridging on the side where posting is not possible

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Lockdown-.title: Lockdown Sweep
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Lockdown-.content:
Control opponents leg tightly

Outer leg goes inward behind the opponents knee
Inner leg locks the leg in place (Similar method as in Triangle)
Inner legs Instep locks the opponents leg in a hook.


Control an arm?
Lift leg and sweep to side where opponent has no possibility to prevent sweep

Uncreated pages

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.title: Ankle pick
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.content:
General
Get a Thumb and cuff grip on the opponents Sleeve on the same side. For example purposes you have gripped left arm with your left (opponents right arm).
Pull downward so opponents steps up and place left leg behind their ankle (this is in order to prevent the backstep)
Pick the opponents ankle with your right hand and raise it to waist level. Left arm is no longer needed and can be positioned to opponents far waist in order to ready for the sweep.
For the the sweep you want to have your hip on opponents upper thigh and simultaneously.

Lift opponent up slightly
Push your waist forward
Twist torso to turn opponent

This should begin the fall and you can follow, but remember to retain the control.

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.title: Arm drag
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.content:
Both arms on opponents single arm and sweeping motion downward, forcing the opponent to lose balance and likely post with knee and/or other hand

Control sleeve with “inner” arm in downward motion and transition to Single leg

“Hopping” around can be stopped by hooking behind the leg



Can be used as part of Ankle pick

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.filePath: Sweeps, throws & takedowns/Standing/Collar & Sleeve to takedown.md
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.title: Collar & Sleeve to takedown
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.content:
Grab a Sleeve and collar grip on the opponent while Standing.
Pull opponent past your Base

So that you have control and they do not


Retain grip on Collar and transition to pseudo Single leg

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.title: Fireman throw
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.content:
Premise
As in the fireman carry the goal is to get opponents weight on your shoulders so that they are in 90 degree angle to you. Then you just sweep them over
What do you need

Upper body control to lower posture to rest on your shoulders
Legs positioned close between the opponents legs

Variations


                  
                  Critique 
                  
                


Unless executed exceptionally there is nothing preventing opponent from taking your back when you try to pull opponent over your shoulders



Under bicep

Start from standing and grab the sleeve under opponents bicep with a grip from the same side (In this example you establish the grip with left hand)
Pull on the grip established and when the opponent reacts by raising their posture put your head close to their armpit (Left side)
At the same time step your right leg’s knee between opponents leg and extend your left to 10’o clock from your starting position
Pull the opponent over you tightly and fall on your ass

Opponent should be move over your head, BUT remember that they need to land in your front arc as otherwise the position will not be advantageous



Upper back

Take cross collar grip with your left hand
Pull and take a high back grip with your left
Threaten a single leg as your left hand can pick up their ankle > Free sweep
Opponent prevents this is what enables the rest of the steps
Pull on the left hand grip on back
Opponent raises posture
Put head on the right side under your arms
Put left leg knee first between their legs + Right leg extended
Pull opponent over you and fall as you did in previous variant

Notes
Two distinct leg positions can be considered

Both knees on ground

Typical wrestling variations, but requires more strength to pull off


Close knee on ground and farther leg extended

Teacher preferred way of teaching it

Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.title: Single leg
Sweeps,-throws--and--takedowns/Standing/.content:
Description
Fairly basic Takedown from Standing often preceded by Shooting or just exposing the leg of opponent by puling them towards you.
All of these moves start with the premise that you have arms reached around one of opponents legs and one knee likely on the ground. Then you raise to a standing while the opponents leg is between yours and the variations begin from there.
For clarification these are described by attacking opponents right leg.
Angle
It is even better if during the leg lifting you can as much of a angle to your opponent as possible. See Base
Head inside
Post in upward fashion with forehead on opponents armpit/ab, this prevents opponent from pushing you down
Regular backwards motion

Retain both hand on the leg
Lower head to opponents thigh
Turn counterclockwise and drop left leg on knee to enable downward momentum as well
The more momentum you have the easier it is to transition to Side control instead of Half guard

Leg pendulum throw

Move right leg to Overhook the opponents leg, freeing your left
Reach around opponents waist with your left hand
Start the motion

Torso moves counter clockwise
Left leg sweeps the opponents Posting leg as low as possible



Head outside


                  
                  Punishable 
                  
                


You cannot retain the head outside for long period as it is dangerous/punishable. This is also prohibited from whitebelts



This position differs largely in that you cannot move opponent “backwards”, but you can drive them to the ground.

Push right elbow on opponents thigh
Attempt to apply downward momentum on the top of their thigh to force them to fall
Retain control of the leg to keep pressure all throughout

Unlinked_Pages.title: Unlinked_Pages
Unlinked_Pages.content:
Unlinked Notes

README
Cross collar grip break to takedown
Collar grip break downward
Grip break on upper back
Double under
Closed guard figure 8
Double ankle grab pass
Knee slice
Toreando
Leg drag
Closed guard standing up
S grip
Buggy choke
Crusifix
Straight ankle lock
Two grip choke on turtle
Close guard overhook stiff
Baseball choke
Lapel choke defence
Side shimmy practice
Neck related
Combat base
K guard
X guards
Turtle to Crusifix
Turtle to Papercutter
Lockdown
High guard
Gi
K
Lasso sweep
De La Riva sweep
Positions of strength
Back escape direction

index.title: BJJ journey
index.content:
Author
I am a 34 year old white belt, who started 08/2023. Due to being a family man I don’t have enough time on the mats, so I’ll vent my excess enthusiasm on writing elaborate notes on everything.
I also work on IT so the CI pipeline and hosting on Github pages was of professional interest as well.
Process
Overall process is loosely based on Zettelkasten methodology, which has been changed to suit my tracking of BJJ journey. The notekeeping started 01/2025 after a on/off training for a bit over a year.
Documentation steps

Focus 100% on the training sessions. Do not plan for what to take notes of
After the training put key ideas and concepts on quick small notes. This can be either post-it notes or voice messages.
Before the next training session write the notes on Obsidian
Commit changes to private Obsidian github repo
Nightly run creates the Quartz site based on the vault contents
Results are pushed to github pages, where you are now looking at the notes

Rules

Only moves or concepts that you’ve tried yourself should be added to the notes.

No insight from Instagram allowed


Process is king and don’t deviate from it
Improve on the contents and refactor a lot

Lists

Unlinked_Pages
Recently_Updated
Empty_Pages

Formatting
Page title #
Segment 
Subsection 


                  
                  Info title 
                  
                


Body





                  
                  Alert title 
                  
                


Body

word_count.title: word_count
word_count.content:
Vault Stats
This vault currently contains 9,555 words across all notes.
